Each database then acquires a lock (indicated by 'lock A' and 'lock C') on its respective data before acknowledging with 'ok' to the coordinator. This constitutes phase 1 ('Prepare').  Once the coordinator receives 'ok' from both databases, it enters phase 2 ('Commit'), sending a commit signal.  Upon receiving the commit signal, each database writes the data, indicated by the red lines representing the actual data write, and releases its lock ('unlock A' and 'unlock C'), sending 'ok' back to the coordinator to confirm successful completion. `,(0,e.jsx)(t.p,{children:"Intuitively, we can create a read-only copy of the database (historical state) and share it with the outside world. Event sourcing answers this question in a slightly different way."}),`
`,(0,e.jsx)(t.p,{children:"Rather than publishing the state (balance information), event sourcing publishes all the events. The external world could rebuild any customized state itself. This design philosophy is called CQRS [11]."}),`
`,(0,e.jsx)(t.p,{children:"In CQRS, there is one state machine responsible for the write part of the state, but there can be many read-only state machines, which are responsible for building views of the states. Those views could be used for queries."}),`
`,(0,e.jsx)(t.p,{children:"These read-only state machines can derive different state representations from the event queue. For example, clients may want to know their balances and a read-only state machine could save state in a database to serve the balance query. Another state machine could build state for a specific time period to help investigate issues like possible double charges. The state information is an audit trail that could help to reconcile the financial records."}),`
`,(0,e.jsx)(t.p,{children:"The read-only state machines lag behind to some extent, but will always catch up. The architecture design is eventually consistent."}),`
`,(0,e.jsx)(t.p,{children:"Figure 19 shows a classic CQRS architecture."}),`
`,(0,e.jsx)(i,{caption:"Figure 19 CQRS architecture",children:(0,e.jsx)(r,{src:j,alt:"Image represents a system architecture divided into a 'Write Path' and a 'Read Path', demonstrating a Command Query Responsibility Segregation (CQRS) pattern with Event Sourcing. The State Machine interacts with a cylinder labeled 'State' (listing balances A:$5, B:$4, C:$3, D:$2) via a '2 Read' arrow and a subsequent '5 Update' arrow originating from a separate State Machine below. The State Machine within the Write Path generates events based on commands and state, indicated by '3' arrows pointing down to the 'Event Queue'. `,(0,e.jsxs)(t.p,{children:[(0,e.jsx)(t.strong,{children:"Candidate"}),": In this design, we use event sourcing architecture to make the whole system reproducible. All valid business records are saved in an immutable Event queue which could be used for correctness verification."]}),`
`,(0,e.jsxs)(t.p,{children:[(0,e.jsx)(t.strong,{children:"Interviewer"}),": That\u2019s great. But the event sourcing architecture you proposed only handles one event at a time and it needs to communicate with several external systems. Can we make it faster?"]}),`
`,(0,e.jsx)(t.h2,{id:"step-3---design-deep-dive",children:"Step 3 - Design Deep Dive"}),`
`,(0,e.jsx)(t.p,{children:"In this section, we dive deep into techniques for achieving high performance, reliability, and scalability."}),`
`,(0,e.jsx)(t.h3,{id:"high-performance-event-sourcing",children:"High-performance event sourcing"}),`
`,(0,e.jsx)(t.p,{children:"In the earlier example, we used Kafka as the command and event store, and the database as a state store. Let\u2019s explore some optimizations."}),`
`,(0,e.jsx)(t.h4,{id:"file-based-command-and-event-list",children:"File-based command and event list"}),`
`,(0,e.jsx)(t.p,{children:"The first optimization is to save commands and events to a local disk, rather than to a remote store like Kafka. This avoids transit time across the network. The event list uses an append-only data structure. Appending is a sequential write operation, which is generally very fast. It works well even for magnetic hard drives because the operating system is heavily optimized for sequential reads and writes. According to this ACM Queue article [12], sequential disk access can be faster than random memory access in some cases."}),`
`,(0,e.jsx)(t.p,{children:"The second optimization is to cache recent commands and events in memory. As we explained before, we process commands and events right after they are persisted. We may cache them in memory to save the time of loading them back from the local disk."}),`
`,(0,e.jsx)(t.p,{children:"We are going to explore some implementation details. A technique called mmap [13] is great for implementing the optimizations mentioned previously. Mmap can write to a local disk and cache recent content in memory at the same time. It maps a disk file to memory as an array. The operating system caches certain sections of the file in memory to accelerate the read and write operations. For append-only file operations, it is almost guaranteed that all data are saved in memory, which is very fast."}),`
`,(0,e.jsx)(t.p,{children:"Figure 20 shows the file-based command and event storage."}),`
`,(0,e.jsx)(i,{caption:"Figure 20 File-based command and event storage",children:(0,e.jsx)(r,{src:A,alt:"Image represents a system architecture diagram illustrating data flow between memory and disk storage. `,(0,e.jsx)(t.h4,{id:"file-based-state",children:"File-based state"}),`

Similar to the optimizations we did for command and event, state information can be saved to the local disk, as well."}),`
`,(0,e.jsx)(t.p,{children:"More specifically, we can use SQLite [14], which is a file-based local relational database or use RocksDB [15], which is a local file-based key-value store."}),`
`,(0,e.jsx)(t.p,{children:"RocksDB is chosen because it uses a log-structured merge-tree (LSM), which is optimized for write operations. To improve read performance, the most recent data is cached."}),`
`,(0,e.jsx)(t.p,{children:"Figure 21 shows the file-based solution for command, event, and state."}),`

`,(0,e.jsx)(t.h4,{id:"snapshot",children:"Snapshot"}),`
`,(0,e.jsx)(t.p,{children:"Once everything is file-based, let us consider how to accelerate the reproducibility process. When we first introduced reproducibility, the state machine had to process events from the very beginning, every time. What we could optimize is to periodically stop the state machine and save the current state into a file. This is called a snapshot."}),`
`,(0,e.jsx)(t.p,{children:"A snapshot is an immutable view of a historical state. Once a snapshot is saved, the state machine does not have to restart from the very beginning anymore. It can read data from a snapshot, verify where it left off, and resume processing from there."}),`
`,(0,e.jsx)(t.p,{children:"For financial applications such as wallet service, the finance team often requires a snapshot to be taken at 00:00 so they can verify all transactions that happened during that day. When we first introduced CQRS of event sourcing, the solution was to set up a read-only state machine that reads from the beginning until the specified time is met. With snapshots, a read-only state machine only needs to load one snapshot that contains the data."}),`
`,(0,e.jsx)(t.p,{children:"A snapshot is a giant binary file and a common solution is to save it in an object storage solution, such as HDFS [16]."}),`
`,(0,e.jsx)(t.p,{children:"Figure 22 shows the file-based event sourcing architecture. When everything is file-based, the system can fully utilize the maximum I/O throughput of the computer hardware."}),`

The system is partitioned into two parts, 'Partition 1 (account A)' and 'Partition 2 (account C),' each containing a Raft node group. Each Raft group consists of three nodes: two followers and one leader (indicated by the double-circle).  Within each partition, the Raft group processes commands (shown flowing from left to right in a pink rectangle) and events (in light blue rectangles). A 'TCC/Saga Coordinator' interacts with the system, sending commands (red arrows) and receiving responses (blue arrows).  The coordinator also maintains a 'Phase Status Table.'  A 'Reverse Proxy' acts as an intermediary, receiving real-time status updates (light blue arrows) pushed from each partition's Raft node group.  Dashed lines indicate data flow within the Raft group, while solid arrows show interactions between components. `,(0,e.jsx)(i,{caption:"Figure 29 Final design in a numbered sequence",children:(0,e.jsx)(r,{src:I,alt:"Image represents a system architecture diagram illustrating a distributed transaction processing system using TCC/Saga and Raft consensus.  The diagram shows two partitions, Partition 1 (account A) and Partition 2 (account C), each containing a Raft Node Group. Each Raft group consists of three nodes: two followers and one leader (indicated by the circular shape with a bold outline).  Each node in the Raft group has 'Events' and 'Commands' components.  A 'TCC/Saga Coordinator' (1) interacts with each partition via a 'Reverse Proxy'.  Commands (3, 9) are sent from the coordinator to the 'Commands' component of the leader node in each partition's Raft group (4, 10).  Events (4, 5, 10, 11) are generated and propagated within each Raft group.  The 'Events' component of the leader node in each partition pushes the latest status to the reverse proxy in real-time (6, 12).  The reverse proxy receives responses (7, 13) from the partitions.  A 'Phase Status Table' (14) is used by the coordinator.  The numbers (1-15) label the components and data flows.  Dashed lines represent asynchronous communication, while solid lines represent synchronous communication.  The system uses a write-then-read pattern, with writes occurring within the Raft group and reads occurring from the database (represented by cylindrical shapes) after successful replication.",width:"700",height:"846"})}),`

`,(0,e.jsx)(t.p,{children:"In this chapter, we designed a wallet service that is capable of processing over 1 million payment commands per second. After a back-of-the-envelope estimation, we concluded that a few thousand nodes are required to support such a load."}),`
`,(0,e.jsx)(t.p,{children:"In the first design, a solution using in-memory key-value stores like Redis is proposed. The problem with this design is that data isn't durable."}),`
`,(0,e.jsx)(t.p,{children:"In the second design, the in-memory cache is replaced by transactional databases. `,(0,e.jsx)(t.p,{children:"Next, event sourcing is introduced. We first implemented event sourcing using an external database and queue, but it\u2019s not performant. We improved performance by storing command, event, and state in a local node."}),`
`,(0,e.jsx)(t.p,{children:"A single node means a single point of failure. To increase the system reliability, we use the Raft consensus algorithm to replicate the event list onto multiple nodes."}),`
`,(0,e.jsx)(t.p,{children:"The last enhancement we made was to adopt the CQRS feature of event sourcing.
